---
title: "Chapter 9: Materials Under Stress"
subtitle: "Reeds Vol 2: Applied Mechanics for Marine Engineers"
---

## Overview

Chapter 9 examines how engineering materials behave when subjected to mechanical loads. It covers the fundamental stress-strain relationships, material properties derived from testing, and the failure criteria relevant to marine engineering components such as shafts, pressure vessels, bolts, and structural members.

---

## Direct Stress and Strain

When an axial force $F$ is applied to a bar of cross-sectional area $A$:

$$\sigma = \frac{F}{A}$$

The resulting strain (change in length per unit length):

$$\varepsilon = \frac{\delta L}{L}$$

**Hooke's Law** states that within the elastic limit, stress is proportional to strain:

$$E = \frac{\sigma}{\varepsilon}$$

The extension of a bar under axial load:

$$\delta L = \frac{FL}{AE}$$

---

## The Tensile Test

The tensile test is the standard method for determining material properties. A specimen is loaded to failure and a **stress-strain curve** is plotted. Key points on the curve:

| Point | Description |
|---|---|
| **Limit of proportionality** | Hooke's Law ceases to apply |
| **Elastic limit** | Beyond this, permanent deformation occurs |
| **Yield point** | Sudden increase in strain with little increase in stress |
| **Ultimate tensile stress (UTS)** | Maximum stress the material can withstand |
| **Fracture point** | Stress at which the specimen breaks |

Mild steel shows a distinct upper and lower yield point; other materials (e.g. aluminium) show a gradual yield and require a **proof stress** (0.1% or 0.2% offset) to define yield strength.

---

## Ductility and Brittleness

- **Ductile materials** (e.g. mild steel, copper) undergo significant plastic deformation before fracture — they give warning of failure
- **Brittle materials** (e.g. cast iron, ceramics) fracture with little or no plastic deformation

**Percentage elongation** and **percentage reduction in area** are standard measures of ductility:

$$\% \text{ elongation} = \frac{L_f - L_0}{L_0} \times 100$$

$$\% \text{ reduction in area} = \frac{A_0 - A_f}{A_0} \times 100$$

---

## Shear Stress and Strain

Shear stress acts tangentially across a plane:

$$\tau = \frac{F}{A}$$

Shear strain $\gamma$ is the angular deformation. Within the elastic limit:

$$G = \frac{\tau}{\gamma}$$

where $G$ is the **Modulus of Rigidity** (shear modulus).

---

## Poisson's Ratio

When a bar is stretched axially, it contracts laterally. Poisson's ratio $\nu$ relates lateral to axial strain:

$$\nu = -\frac{\varepsilon_{\text{lateral}}}{\varepsilon_{\text{axial}}}$$

Typical values: $\nu \approx 0.25$–$0.33$ for metals.

---

## Volumetric Strain and Bulk Modulus

Under uniform hydrostatic pressure $p$, the volumetric strain is:

$$\varepsilon_v = \frac{\delta V}{V} = \frac{p}{K}$$

where $K$ is the **Bulk Modulus**.

---

## Relationships Between Elastic Constants

The three elastic constants are linked through Poisson's ratio:

$$E = 2G(1 + \nu)$$

$$E = 3K(1 - 2\nu)$$

---

## Thermal Stress

If a bar is **restrained from expanding** when its temperature rises by $\Delta T$, a compressive stress is induced:

$$\sigma = E\alpha\Delta T$$

where $\alpha$ is the coefficient of linear thermal expansion. This is particularly important in pipework, cylinder liners, and any component subject to temperature cycling.

For a **composite bar** (e.g. steel bolt through a brass tube), compatibility of deformation and force equilibrium are solved simultaneously to find the stress in each material.

---

## Stress Concentration

At holes, notches, keyways, and sudden changes of section, the local stress exceeds the nominal stress by a **stress concentration factor** $K_t$:

$$\sigma_{\max} = K_t \cdot \sigma_{\text{nom}}$$

Stress concentrations are a common cause of fatigue failure in marine shafting and structural components.

---

## Factor of Safety

The factor of safety relates the failure stress to the permissible working stress:

$$\text{FoS} = \frac{\text{failure stress}}{\text{working stress}}$$

The appropriate failure stress may be the **yield stress** (for ductile materials) or the **UTS** (for brittle materials), depending on the design philosophy.

---

## Hardness Testing

Hardness is a measure of a material's resistance to surface indentation. Common tests include:

- **Brinell** — steel ball indenter, used for soft to medium-hard materials
- **Vickers** — diamond pyramid indenter, wide range of materials
- **Rockwell** — rapid testing, common in industry

Hardness correlates approximately with UTS for steels:

$$\text{UTS (MPa)} \approx 3.3 \times \text{Brinell Hardness Number (BHN)}$$

---

## Impact Testing

Impact tests measure a material's resistance to sudden (dynamic) loading — its **toughness**.

- **Charpy test** — notched specimen struck by a swinging pendulum; energy absorbed is measured
- **Izod test** — similar principle, different specimen geometry

Toughness is especially important in marine environments where low temperatures can cause a **ductile-to-brittle transition** in steels.

---

## Fatigue

Repeated cyclic loading causes failure at stresses well below the static UTS. The **endurance limit** (or fatigue limit) is the stress below which a material can endure an unlimited number of cycles without failure.

Key factors affecting fatigue life:
- Stress concentration
- Surface finish
- Corrosive environment (particularly significant at sea)
- Mean stress level

---

## Summary of Equations

| Concept | Equation |
|---|---|
| Direct stress | $\sigma = F/A$ |
| Direct strain | $\varepsilon = \delta L / L$ |
| Young's modulus | $E = \sigma/\varepsilon$ |
| Bar extension | $\delta L = FL/AE$ |
| Shear modulus | $G = \tau/\gamma$ |
| Poisson's ratio | $\nu = -\varepsilon_{\text{lat}}/\varepsilon_{\text{axial}}$ |
| Elastic constants | $E = 2G(1+\nu) = 3K(1-2\nu)$ |
| Thermal stress | $\sigma = E\alpha\Delta T$ |
| Stress concentration | $\sigma_{\max} = K_t \cdot \sigma_{\text{nom}}$ |
| Hardness–UTS (steel) | UTS $\approx 3.3 \times$ BHN |

---

## Summary

Understanding material behaviour under stress is fundamental to safe marine engineering design. The tensile test provides the key properties — Young's modulus, yield stress, UTS, and ductility — while fatigue, impact toughness, and stress concentration govern real-world failure in service. The harsh marine environment (corrosion, temperature extremes, dynamic loading) makes material selection and the choice of an appropriate factor of safety especially critical.
